questions.question_text: Under New York law, which body has the authority to enact local zoning ordinances?
distractors: The state legislature through direct enactment
The county planning board
The zoning board of appeals
questions.explanation: New York delegates zoning power to municipalities through three parallel enabling statutes. Towns act under Town Law Article 16, villages under Village Law Article 7, and cities under General City Law Article 2-A. The local legislative body, such as a town board or city council, adopts and amends zoning regulations, while the ZBA interprets and grants relief from zoning but does not create the ordinances.

questions.question_text: A property owner has operated a small grocery store in a residential zone for 20 years. The municipality now rezones the area to prohibit commercial use. What happens to the grocery store?
questions.correct_answer: It continues as a legal non-conforming use
distractors: It must close within 90 days of the rezoning
It may expand to include additional retail space
It requires a new special use permit to operate
questions.explanation: A pre-existing use that was lawful before a zoning change is grandfathered as a legal non-conforming use. The owner may continue operating but generally cannot expand or substantially alter the non-conforming use. If the use is abandoned or discontinued, it typically cannot be resumed.

questions.question_text: Which municipal body is responsible for developing the comprehensive master plan and approving subdivision applications?
distractors: The zoning board of appeals
The town or city council
The building department
questions.explanation: The planning board is an appointed body that develops the community's comprehensive or master plan and reviews subdivision and site plan applications. The ZBA handles variances and appeals of zoning decisions, while the legislative body enacts the zoning ordinances themselves.

questions.question_text: A homeowner wants to build an accessory dwelling unit in a zone that permits only single-family residences. The ZBA denies a variance. Which board should the homeowner petition if they believe the zoning itself should change?
questions.correct_answer: The municipal legislative body for a rezoning
distractors: The planning board for a master plan override
The architectural review board for design approval
The county health department for a waiver
questions.explanation: If a variance is denied and the property owner believes the zoning classification itself is inappropriate, the proper remedy is to petition the municipal legislative body for a rezoning or amendment to the zoning ordinance. The ZBA can only grant relief within existing zoning, not change the zoning itself.

questions.question_text: A church wants to build in a residential zone where religious institutions are allowed with municipal approval. What type of permit is needed?
questions.correct_answer: A special use permit from the ZBA
distractors: A use variance based on unnecessary hardship
An area variance for the building footprint
No permit because religious use is a constitutional right
questions.explanation: A special use permit, also called a conditional use permit, allows a use that the zoning ordinance specifically contemplates for the district but requires individual review and approval. Unlike a variance, a special use permit does not require a showing of hardship because the use is already conditionally permitted in the zone.

questions.question_text: To obtain a use variance in New York, the applicant must demonstrate which standard?
questions.correct_answer: Unnecessary hardship depriving all economic use
distractors: Practical difficulty meeting dimensional requirements
Financial loss compared to neighboring properties
Community benefit outweighing any negative impact
questions.explanation: A use variance in New York requires the applicant to prove unnecessary hardship: that the property cannot yield a reasonable return under any permitted use, the hardship is unique to the property, the variance will not alter the essential character of the neighborhood, and the hardship is not self-created.

questions.question_text: A homeowner applies for an area variance to build a deck that extends two feet into the required setback. The ZBA learns the homeowner knew about the setback when purchasing the property. Can the variance still be granted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, self-created hardship does not bar an area variance
distractors: No, self-created hardship automatically disqualifies the request
Only if the homeowner agrees to reduce the deck by one foot
Only if all adjacent property owners provide written consent
questions.explanation: For area variances in New York, self-created hardship is one factor in the balancing test but does not automatically preclude granting the variance. This differs from use variances, where self-created hardship can defeat the application. The ZBA weighs practical difficulty against the impact on the neighborhood.

questions.question_text: A developer proposes a 200-unit residential subdivision. Under SEQRA, which entity determines whether an environmental impact statement is required?
distractors: The state Department of Environmental Conservation exclusively
The project developer's environmental consultant
The local building inspector
questions.explanation: Under the State Environmental Quality Review Act (SEQRA), the lead agency is the governmental body most directly responsible for the project approval. The lead agency determines the significance of environmental impacts and whether a full Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) is required.

questions.question_text: What is the key legal standard the ZBA applies when evaluating an area variance application?
questions.correct_answer: A balancing test weighing practical difficulty
distractors: Proof the owner is deprived of all economic use
Demonstration that the hardship is not self-created
Evidence the proposed use benefits the entire community
questions.explanation: Area variances use a practical difficulty standard with a balancing test that weighs factors including the benefit to the applicant, impact on the neighborhood, whether the difficulty is substantial, whether alternatives exist, and whether the difficulty is self-created. This is a less stringent standard than the unnecessary hardship test for use variances.

questions.question_text: A property owner wants to convert a single-family home to a two-family residence in a zone that only permits single-family use. The owner claims no reasonable return is possible without the conversion. What type of relief is the owner seeking?
distractors: An area variance
A special use permit
A non-conforming use certificate
questions.explanation: Changing from a permitted use (single-family) to a different use (two-family) requires a use variance. The owner must prove unnecessary hardship, including that the property cannot yield a reasonable return under any permitted use, a much higher burden than the practical difficulty standard for area variances.

questions.question_text: A property owner believes the ZBA acted arbitrarily in denying a variance application. Under New York law, what is the correct legal proceeding to challenge the decision?
questions.correct_answer: An Article 78 proceeding in state court
distractors: A direct appeal to the planning board
A federal civil rights lawsuit under Section 1983
A petition to the state legislature
questions.explanation: An Article 78 proceeding under the New York Civil Practice Law and Rules is the proper mechanism to challenge quasi-judicial decisions of local agencies, including ZBA determinations. The court reviews whether the decision was arbitrary, capricious, or an abuse of discretion.

questions.question_text: Which New York law requires that meetings of municipal planning boards and ZBAs be open to the public?
distractors: The Freedom of Information Law (FOIL)
SEQRA notification requirements
The Municipal Home Rule Law
questions.explanation: New York's Open Meetings Law, commonly called the Sunshine Law, requires that meetings of public bodies, including planning boards and ZBAs, be open to the public. FOIL governs access to government records, which is a related but separate transparency requirement.

questions.question_text: A newly constructed home needs final approval before occupancy. Which document must the building department issue?
distractors: A building permit amendment
A planning board site plan approval
A zoning compliance letter
questions.explanation: A certificate of occupancy (CO) is issued by the building department after a final inspection confirms the structure complies with building codes and the approved plans. Without a CO, a property generally cannot be legally occupied, and lenders typically will not close on a purchase.

questions.question_text: An applicant proposes installing a septic system for a new home in an area without municipal sewer service. Which agency typically reviews and approves the septic system plan?
distractors: The municipal planning board
The zoning board of appeals
The state Department of Environmental Conservation
questions.explanation: In New York, county health departments typically have jurisdiction over individual sewage disposal systems, including septic tanks and leach fields. The health department reviews soil percolation tests and system design to ensure the system will not contaminate groundwater or create public health hazards.

questions.question_text: A municipality wants to evaluate the visual impact of new commercial buildings on a historic downtown. Which local body typically reviews architectural design standards?
distractors: The zoning board of appeals
The building department inspector
The county planning commission
questions.explanation: Architectural review boards (ARBs) evaluate the design, scale, and aesthetic compatibility of proposed construction within designated areas. They are advisory or regulatory depending on local law, and they focus on appearance rather than zoning compliance or structural integrity.
